explicit_text: I enlisted in the United States Marine Corps in 1948, right after World War II had ended but while the occupation was still ongoing. My cousin married a Marine, which influenced my decision, and I went through boot camp at Parris Island. The Corps has a strong esprit de corps, built through tough training, singing the Marine Corps hymn, and learning about its rich history, including battles like Derna and Veracruz. Serving in the Marines shaped my life and philosophy deeply.

This service has a strong esprit de corps, built through tough training, singing its hymn, and learning about its rich history, including battles like Derna and Veracruz. Serving in this military branch shaped my life and philosophy deeply.

This connection to the Mize family was part of what made the Marine who married my cousin stand out to me. It was a small but interesting link between my family and sports history while I was joining the Marines in 1948.

explicit_text: I went to boot camp at Parris Island, South Carolina, after enlisting in 1948. It was there that I experienced the intense orientation and de-orientation process that transformed civilians into Marines. We marched, drilled, did calisthenics, and learned the Marine Corps hymn, all under the watchful eyes of our instructors. Parris Island was where I first truly became a Marine.

explicit_text: San Diego was the other main location for Marine boot camp, especially for recruits west of the Mississippi River. While I went to Parris Island on the east coast, those from the west would have gone to San Diego. It’s part of the Marine Corps’ training infrastructure, complementing Parris Island.

explicit_text: In 1948, the British were still rationing bread and clothes, even after the war had ended. This was a fact we were told during training, highlighting how tough post-war conditions were in Europe. It gave me perspective on the global aftermath of World War II while I was serving in the Marines.

explicit_text: November 10 is the United States Marine Corps birthday, a big celebration every year because the Corps was established in 1775. This date is very important to every Marine, and we honor it with ceremonies and remembrance. It connects us to the long history and traditions of the Corps.

explicit_text: The Battle of Derna was a key event during the Barbary Wars when Marines, led by William Eaton and supported by Greek mercenaries, marched across North Africa to attack the city of Derna in Libya. This battle was part of President Jefferson’s efforts to stop Barbary pirates from attacking American ships. The Marines stormed the city, raised the flag, and secured a victory that still resonates in our hymn and history.

The Marines stormed the city, raised the flag, and secured a victory that still resonates in our hymn and history.

explicit_text: The Barbary Wars were fought in the early 1800s to stop North African pirates from capturing American ships and enslaving crews. President Thomas Jefferson commissioned naval forces and Marines to confront these pirates, leading to battles like the one at Derna. This conflict showed the Marines’ early role as protectors of American interests overseas.

He was more interested in money than diplomacy, which led to conflict with the United States. When Eaton and the Marines attacked Derna, the Shah fled, and his forces were defeated, ending his harassment of American ships.

explicit_text: The Battle of Veracruz was part of the Mexican–American War when Marines landed and fought to capture the city. This battle is remembered in the Marine Corps hymn as the 'Halls of Montezuma.' It was a tough fight that ended with the Americans raising their flag over Mexico City, marking a significant Marine engagement.

This battle is remembered in the branch’s hymn as the 'Halls of Montezuma.' It was a tough fight that ended with the Americans raising their flag over Mexico City, marking a significant Marine engagement.

explicit_text: Mexico City was captured by American forces, including Marines, during the Mexican–American War after battles like Veracruz. The Marines fought their way through and raised the flag in the city, symbolizing victory over the Aztec Empire. This event is commemorated in the Marine Corps hymn and is a proud part of Marine history.
